lens.included_activities: Independent, cooperative, and custom cotton-ginning establishments that repeatedly separate lint from seed and perform associated drying, cleaning, contamination control, baling, and shipment preparation for external growers or cotton owners.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive ginning embedded in a vertically integrated farming operation, inactive plants, cotton farming, cottonseed processing, warehousing without ginning, and machinery or software vendors.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Seasonal business-to-business processing fees paid by growers, cooperatives, or cotton owners, generally linked to bales or volume handled, with possible ancillary revenue from seed handling, storage, and related services.

a.bridge: USDA descriptions of gin operations and demonstrated machine-vision systems identify automatable inspection, contamination detection, cleaner control, process optimization, maintenance prediction, scheduling, and administrative work. The estimate converts those use cases into a share of total labor cost while discounting physical unloading, machinery operation, repair, baling, safety, and accountable supervision; it may overstate adoption-ready automation in older small plants.
a.rationale: Ginning is already machinery-intensive, so AI adds most value through sensing, decisions, optimization, and back-office coordination rather than replacement of the physical process. The remaining manual, maintenance, safety, and peak-season operating work keeps the automatable share well below one-half.
a.change_evidence: Plant-level occupational hours and task studies split among process operation, maintenance, material handling, quality inspection, dispatch, and administration, paired with measured hours saved after AI deployment.
caveats: No cotton-ginning-specific task-time survey was located.
The direct commercial machine-vision evidence is older and does not establish current fleet-wide deployment.

rho.bridge: USDA reports that cotton-gin machine vision can monitor trash, control cleaners, and detect plastic contamination, establishing technical feasibility for several high-value sensing tasks. Realizable savings are discounted for seasonal utilization, legacy-machine integration, false-detection risk, capital constraints, and continued need for operators and maintenance; the proxy could overstate realization for small or low-throughput gins.
rho.rationale: The core use cases are technically credible and some can directly control equipment, but converting technical capability into recurring labor savings depends on retrofit economics, throughput, reliability, and operational integration.
rho.change_evidence: Current vendor or operator case studies reporting installation cost, uptime, false-positive rates, labor-hour reduction, and payback across representative gin sizes.
caveats: Technical capability is not equivalent to economical realization.
Seasonal plants may have fewer annual hours over which to amortize automation.

e.rationale: Most independent and cooperative gins provide recurring processing services to external growers or cotton owners and therefore fit the lens, while captive integrated capacity, inactive plants, and firms outside the target size band reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A current establishment roster showing ownership, cooperative status, active capacity, customer mix, and revenue for every gin.
caveats: The active-gin census does not identify ownership or whether revenue falls in the target band.
Cooperative and vertically integrated structures blur the external-customer boundary.

s5.rationale: The fall in active gins and the concentration of throughput in larger plants support some consolidation and succession opportunity, but the small target-firm count, cooperative ownership, specialized assets, and local grower relationships limit the share likely to transact within five years.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of cotton-gin acquisitions, closures, ownership transfers, broker listings, owner ages, and cooperative conversion records matched to the target revenue band.
caveats: Closures are not necessarily acquisitions or succession events.
No comprehensive transaction series for private cotton gins was located.

q.rationale: Local transport economics, installed capacity, trusted grower relationships, throughput reliability, and quality control should allow a capable gin to retain part of AI-enabled savings. Capture is constrained by cooperative pass-through, price competition, commodity cycles, excess local capacity, and the possibility that growers demand lower fees.
q.change_evidence: Matched before-and-after gross margins, fee schedules, capacity utilization, local competitor density, and customer retention for gins adopting automation.
caveats: Cooperative economics can intentionally return savings to members rather than retain them as firm profit.
Local competitive structure varies sharply across cotton regions.

d5.bridge: National running bales ginned are the closest observed volume proxy for service demand. The 2025 census reports 13.5 million bales, down 4 percent from 2024, while active gins fell from 446 to 419; extrapolating cautiously over five years yields near-flat base demand with a wide weather-, acreage-, yield-, export-, and regional-share range. National volume may overstate an individual gin's durability where local acreage shifts or competitors consolidate capacity.
d5.rationale: Cotton must be ginned after harvest, making service demand tightly linked to crop volume, but annual output is volatile and long-run acreage faces commodity-price, export, weather, water, and competing-crop uncertainty. Consolidation can support surviving plants' throughput even when the national gin count declines.
d5.change_evidence: USDA regional acreage, yield, production, export, and mill-use forecasts through 2031 combined with county-level gin capacity and closures.
caveats: One year of national change is not a five-year forecast.
National bale volume can diverge materially from the serviceable volume of a particular local gin.

o.rationale: Even highly automated gins require a legally and operationally accountable operator for safety, equipment reliability, crop custody, quality decisions, customer relationships, and exception handling. Consolidation may reduce the number of firms but is unlikely to eliminate the operator function at a surviving plant.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of fully autonomous commercial gins, remote-operation regulation, insurer requirements, and observed post-automation staffing and ownership structures.
caveats: The construct concerns persistence of an accountable operator, not preservation of every current job.
Remote supervision could change the organizational form without eliminating accountability.

narrative.executive_view: Cotton ginning is a narrow, essential, machinery-intensive agricultural service with tangible AI opportunities in sensing, contamination control, process settings, maintenance, and administration. Its appeal is tempered by seasonality, a small and consolidating establishment base, volatile crop volumes, and cooperative or customer bargaining that can pass savings through.
narrative.ai_impact: AI is most likely to augment an already automated physical process: camera systems can inspect lint and contaminants, models can tune drying and cleaning, and predictive tools can improve maintenance, scheduling, dispatch, and paperwork. Material handling, repair, safety oversight, and exception response remain physical and site-specific.
narrative.value_capture: A high-throughput gin with local density and reliable quality can retain some labor, downtime, and quality gains. Capture weakens when utilization is low, retrofit costs are high, competitors have spare capacity, or cooperative governance returns savings to growers.
narrative.firm_availability: The market is small: USDA counted 419 active gins in 2025, and the injected target-band firm count is only 32. Eligibility is further narrowed by inactive or captive plants, cooperative structures, and ownership or revenue ambiguity, while declining gin counts suggest both consolidation and a thin acquisition pool.
narrative.demand_durability: Every commercially harvested cotton crop requires ginning, supporting the service's functional necessity. Five-year volume remains uncertain because acreage, yield, weather, water, exports, mill use, and regional shifts can dominate; consolidation may nevertheless increase throughput at surviving plants.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of cotton-gin-specific occupational task shares, current adoption and payback data, ownership-resolved establishment data, and transaction evidence. Crop volatility, seasonal utilization, legacy integration, contamination-model accuracy, local capacity, and cooperative pass-through widen the outcomes.
narrative.principal_driver: Indispensable local processing combined with measurable machine-vision and process-control use cases creates a credible path to operational improvement at surviving high-throughput gins.
narrative.principal_weakness: A tiny, consolidating firm universe and volatile cotton volumes make firm availability and durable utilization materially less certain than the technical automation opportunity.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 11, NAICS 115111 Cotton Ginning
sources.supports: Exact industry definition: establishments primarily engaged in ginning cotton.

sources.title: What Is a Cotton Gin?
sources.supports: Core process, including lint-seed separation, drying, cleaning, foreign-matter removal, equipment adjustment, maintenance, and throughput considerations.

sources.title: Machine Vision Automating Cotton Gins
sources.supports: Commercial machine-vision use for trash monitoring and automatic cleaner control, demonstrating direct automation feasibility and quality benefits.

sources.title: Machine-vision detection and removal of plastic contamination in cotton gins
sources.supports: Low-cost color-camera and air-jet system for automated contamination detection and removal.

sources.title: Cotton Ginnings Survey
sources.supports: The survey is a census of active gins across 17 cotton states and records bales, lint weight, average bale weight, and plants throughout the season.

sources.title: Cotton Ginnings 2025 Summary
sources.supports: Reports 13.5 million running bales ginned in 2025, down 4 percent from 2024; 419 active gins versus 446 in 2024; and 60 percent of active gins processing more than 20,000 bales.
